primary_diagnosis.name: Suspected Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome (accessory-pathway pre-excitation) presenting with recurrent, self-terminating paroxysmal AVRT; currently in sinus rhythm and haemodynamically stable post-episode

pathophysiology_summary: Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome is a form of ventricular pre-excitation in which an accessory atrioventricular pathway (an additional muscular connection present from birth) conducts alongside the AV node. On a resting 12-lead ECG this classically produces a short PR interval (<120 ms), a slurred initial upstroke of the QRS (delta wave) and a widened QRS - the pathognomonic, sealed diagnostic finding. Clinically it predisposes to paroxysmal atrioventricular re-entrant tachycardia (AVRT): an abrupt-onset, abrupt-offset ('switch-like'), perfectly regular, very fast re-entrant tachycardia, typically in a young, structurally normal heart, often producing presyncope. These features distinguish it from sinus tachycardia (gradual, rate proportionate to stimulus), from a panic attack (usually a more gradual crescendo of anxiety symptoms rather than an instantaneous switch-like onset/offset with presyncope), and from atrial fibrillation (which is irregularly irregular). The single most important clinical-safety point is pre-excited atrial fibrillation: if a WPW patient develops AF, the impulse can conduct rapidly down the accessory pathway and, crucially, AV-nodal blocking drugs - adenosine, verapamil/diltiazem, digoxin and beta-blockers - can paradoxically accelerate accessory-pathway conduction and precipitate ventricular fibrillation and sudden cardiac death, so those agents are CONTRAINDICATED in pre-excited AF. In this presentation the tachycardia has already self-terminated (as in prior episodes), the patient is now in sinus rhythm, conversing and haemodynamically stable, so there is no acute rhythm to terminate; the priority is a resting 12-lead ECG to look for the delta wave / short PR pre-excitation pattern and referral for a cardiology/electrophysiology (EP) assessment. Definitive treatment is EP study with accessory-pathway catheter ablation, which is often curative. None of the diagnostic ECG can be obtained over telehealth; a modified Valsalva can be safely coached as first-line self-help for a future episode, but no drug treatment should be recommended remotely, and a future episode that fails to self-terminate, causes true syncope, or presents as a fast irregular rhythm requires emergency assessment.

differential.diagnosis: Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia due to accessory-pathway pre-excitation (suspected Wolff-Parkinson-White / AVRT)
differential.evidence_basis: Abrupt-onset AND abrupt-offset ('switch-like'), perfectly regular, very fast palpitations at rest with presyncope, recurrent in a young healthy person, are classic for re-entrant paroxysmal SVT, and the recurrent switch-like pattern raises accessory-pathway pre-excitation (WPW).
The patient is anxious, but a panic attack usually builds and eases gradually and does not typically cause a switch-like instantaneous onset/offset with presyncope forcing her to lie flat.

Considered for episodic palpitations, but the perfectly regular character argues against the irregularly irregular pulse of AF (though pre-excited AF must be kept in mind as a dangerous future possibility in a WPW patient).
Must be considered for exertional/arrhythmic symptoms, though good exercise tolerance and a structurally normal heart make it less likely; an ECG and, if indicated, echocardiography help exclude it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the outset the AI Doctor should treat switch-like regular presyncopal palpitations in a young person as probable paroxysmal SVT/pre-excitation, not simply anxiety, and arrange a priority resting ECG.

differential.diagnosis: Suspected Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome (accessory-pathway pre-excitation) with recurrent self-terminating paroxysmal AVRT, currently resolved and stable
differential.evidence_basis: Switch-like abrupt onset/offset regular tachycardia (DI-001) with presyncope (DI-002), recurrent in a healthy young person (DI-003), now fully self-resolved and stable (DI-004), strongly indicates recurrent paroxysmal SVT from an accessory pathway needing a priority resting ECG to confirm a pre-excitation pattern and EP referral.
Instantaneous switch-like onset/offset with presyncope forcing her to lie flat is not a typical panic attack.
The described rhythm is regular (arguing against AF now), but pre-excited AF remains an important dangerous future possibility in a WPW patient and shapes drug-safety advice.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is stable, self-resolved recurrent paroxysmal SVT with suspected pre-excitation: arrange a priority resting 12-lead ECG and EP referral, coach a modified Valsalva for future episodes, and safety-net to 000 for a non-terminating or syncopal recurrence.

differential.diagnosis: Suspected Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome / paroxysmal AVRT pending a priority resting 12-lead ECG (short PR, delta wave) and cardiology/electrophysiology assessment
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. The delta wave / short PR pre-excitation pattern can only be confirmed on a 12-lead ECG; the correct action is a priority resting ECG and EP referral, with a modified Valsalva coached for future episodes and emergency escalation if a future episode does not self-terminate, causes true syncope, or is a fast irregular rhythm.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is suspected pre-excitation (WPW) with recurrent paroxysmal SVT - now resolved and stable - requiring a priority resting ECG and EP referral, trigger avoidance, a coached vagal manoeuvre for the future, and clear safety-netting; it must never be cleared as benign anxiety.

red_flags.description: Abrupt-onset AND abrupt-offset ('switch-like'), regular, very rapid tachycardia at rest, recurrent in a young person
red_flags.clinical_significance: This pattern is characteristic of a re-entrant paroxysmal SVT and, given recurrence in a young structurally-normal heart, raises accessory-pathway pre-excitation (WPW) requiring a priority ECG.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Dismissed as anxiety; the pre-excitation pattern is never captured and the patient remains at risk in a future episode (including a dangerous pre-excited AF).

red_flags.description: Presyncope during the tachycardia (nearly passed out, had to lie flat) with chest tightness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Presyncope reflects impaired cardiac output at very high rates and marks the arrhythmia as clinically significant, warranting prompt (priority) investigation even though it has resolved.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognising the haemodynamic impact leads to under-investigation of a potentially dangerous arrhythmia.

red_flags.description: Features that would indicate an unstable or dangerous future episode (a non-terminating episode, actual syncope, severe crushing chest pain, or a fast irregular rhythm) - currently ABSENT but must be safety-netted
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their current absence (episode self-terminated, no true syncope, no crushing pain, rhythm was regular) supports stable outpatient work-up; if any develop in a future episode the correct action is emergency care (000), and in a fast irregular pre-excited rhythm AV-nodal blocking drugs are contraindicated.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If a future unstable or pre-excited-AF episode is mismanaged (e.g. an AV-nodal blocker given), it can accelerate accessory-pathway conduction and precipitate ventricular fibrillation and sudden death.

red_flags.description: Short PR interval (<120 ms), delta wave (slurred QRS upstroke) and widened QRS on resting 12-lead ECG (pre-excitation / WPW pattern)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The delta wave / short PR on a 12-lead ECG is the pathognomonic, sealed diagnostic finding that confirms pre-excitation and defines the accessory pathway; it cannot be obtained over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - it requires a 12-lead ECG; the correct action is a priority resting ECG and cardiology/EP referral for accessory-pathway ablation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Attributing it to anxiety/panic or caffeine alone
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The patient is anxious and uses stimulants, but a switch-like instantaneous onset/offset regular tachycardia with presyncope is a re-entrant SVT, not simple anxiety or sinus tachycardia, and needs a priority ECG to look for pre-excitation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Falsely reassuring because the episode has resolved
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Self-termination is typical of paroxysmal SVT and does not remove the need for a priority ECG and specialist review; premature closure here misses WPW.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Recommending AV-nodal blocking self-medication for future episodes
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Adenosine, verapamil/diltiazem, digoxin and beta-blockers are contraindicated in pre-excited AF because they can accelerate accessory-pathway conduction and precipitate VF; no AV-nodal blocker should be recommended for home self-treatment before the ECG and specialist assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Not coaching a safe vagal manoeuvre or not safety-netting a future episode
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A modified Valsalva is a safe first-line self-help step for a future stable episode, and clear 000 escalation criteria (non-terminating episode, true syncope, severe chest pain, fast irregular rhythm) must be given.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Resting 12-lead ECG to identify a pre-excitation pattern (short PR interval, delta wave, widened QRS)
Structural cardiovascular examination (heart sounds, murmurs, right ventricular heave, peripheral pulses)
Rhythm capture during an episode (a 12-lead ECG in tachycardia)
Electrophysiology study to localise the accessory pathway
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Because the episode has self-terminated and the patient is stable and in sinus rhythm, arrange a priority resting 12-lead ECG (urgent, same-day/next-morning) to look for a pre-excitation pattern and refer to cardiology/electrophysiology for assessment and potential accessory-pathway ablation; coach a modified Valsalva for a future episode, advise avoidance of stimulants/energy drinks, and safety-net to call 000 if a future episode does not self-terminate, causes actual syncope, or comes with severe chest pain.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient that the episodes are 'just anxiety' or a benign one-off because they self-resolve, and not arranging a priority ECG, risks missing pre-excitation (WPW) and leaves her exposed to a dangerous future episode - including pre-excited atrial fibrillation in which AV-nodal blocking drugs can precipitate ventricular fibrillation.
